text.title: Business Process Analysis: Enterprise Scale and Governance
text.summary: Portfolio-agent orchestration pattern for enterprise BPA, BPMN governance, reference-framework alignment, and M&A/ERP specializations.

section.title: Portfolio-Agent Orchestration

rule.statement: Run enterprise BPA as two-tier orchestration: a portfolio agent that maintains the inventory and governance artifacts, plus per-process agents (one invocation per in-scope process) applying the 5-stage loop.
rule.rationale: A single agent attempting to model 100+ processes in one context produces shallow, hallucinated artifacts. The portfolio agent acts as coordinator — it assigns scope, enforces the BPMN standard, and rolls up metrics; per-process agents produce the deliverables.

rule.statement: Limit deep-modeling to the top decile by nva_minutes_per_year × strategic_fit; document all other processes at narrative depth (one paragraph each).
rule.rationale: Full documentation of a 500-process landscape is unmaintainable; the Pareto principle applied to NVA impact identifies the 10% of processes delivering 80% of improvement value.

section.reference: Ranks processes by NVA x feasibility composite score from scorecard.csv

rule.statement: Decide level-of-detail (L1/L2/L3/L4), naming convention (verb-noun), pool/lane discipline, and message-flow rules before any modelling starts; put the rules in standards/bpmn-conventions.md and have every agent read them.
rule.rationale: BPMN files produced without a shared dialect are incompatible at the portfolio level; a governance board that approves diagrams with inconsistent conventions creates technical debt in the model repository.

rule.statement: Treat BPMN as code: source-control XML, run bpmnlint in CI, code-review changes, tag releases per programme milestone.
rule.rationale: BPMN files without version history cannot answer "what changed and why?" — the audit trail required by ISO 9001 and SOX 404 auditors.

rule.statement: Anchor to APQC PCF, SCOR, or eTOM as scaffolding, not law; for every deviation from the reference, capture rationale and competitive-differentiation hypothesis.
rule.rationale: Reference-model fundamentalism forces genuine differentiators into generic boxes, destroying competitive advantage documentation. The deviation log is the intellectual property of the BPA exercise.

antipattern.description: Lifting APQC PCF text verbatim as if it describes the actual company processes.
antipattern.reason: Reference processes are industry averages; the company's reality may differ significantly. Every reference-derived sentence must be tagged [reference] and reviewed by a human before publication.

rule.statement: Maintain a per-L2-process ownership decision log with entries: acquirer | target | hybrid | new, each citing a deal-thesis line; un-cited decisions are blocked.
rule.rationale: M&A process decisions made without deal-thesis grounding reflect political convenience rather than value creation; the citation requirement enforces discipline and provides audit evidence.

example.note: Portfolio agent kickoff prompt for M&A integration enforces structured output without inventing process owners.
example.code: Inputs: acquirer-pcf.csv, target-pcf.csv, deal-thesis.md, integration-priorities.md. Build the Process Portfolio Comparison artifact: - Map both inventories to APQC PCF Level 2. - For every PCF L2 row: acquirer_owner, target_owner, decision (adopt-acquirer | adopt-target | hybrid | new), rationale (cite deal-thesis line). - Flag any L2 with no owner on either side as "GAP — requires design". - Output: portfolio.csv + decisions-log.md. Do NOT model individual processes yet.

rule.statement: For ERP gap-fit analysis, follow: adopt-standard → configure → extend → build-custom → drop; document each gap with explicit decision type. Agents bias toward "build" — counter with explicit configure-first prompts.
rule.rationale: Every customization multiplies upgrade cost; the adopt/configure priority order ensures the lowest-cost option is evaluated before custom development is authorized.

antipattern.description: Cross-country process smoothing: treating a multi-jurisdiction process as one global flow.
antipattern.reason: Tax, payroll, and labor law vary significantly by country; a global process diagram that hides legal variants creates compliance risk and failed implementations.
